parent.label: Legal & Government
parent.description: Court opinions, statutes, regulations, patents, and government documents. Legal writing has a distinctive structure: citations, precedent, numbered provisions, 'whereas' clauses, and very careful attention to the exact meaning of words. Government documents include everything from tax forms to Congressional records to administrative agency rulemakings.
bullets: FreeLaw / CourtListener: 7M+ federal and state court opinions
US Patent and Trademark Office: 11M+ patents going back to 1836
Federal Register: 85+ years of regulatory rulemaking in formal prose

cells.label: Court Opinions
cells.description: FreeLaw (CourtListener) and the Public Access to Court Electronic Records (PACER) system — millions of federal and state court opinions. Legal reasoning is extraordinarily precise about how language is used: a comma in a statute has been the subject of multimillion-dollar litigation.
bullets: CourtListener: 7M+ court documents, full text, from all federal circuits
Legal citations teach models formal reference structure: 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
Judicial writing teaches models argument from precedent and distinguishing cases
cells.screenshotCaption: Justia — one of the public portals for freely-accessible US court opinions

cells.label: Patents
cells.description: Patents have a unique genre: independent claims, dependent claims, prior art, and specification sections. They teach models highly structured technical description with specific legal requirements about what must be disclosed and how broadly a claim may be drawn.
bullets: USPTO: 11M+ issued patents, full text searchable back to 1976
Patent claims require precise, exhaustive enumeration of every meaningful component
Prior art sections describe existing technology in controlled, citable form
cells.screenshotCaption: Google Patents — a public interface for USPTO and international patent full text
